topic: Geographic Themes (Three from the SL list)
subject: geography

items.q: Do HL geography students study all of the optional themes on the list?
items.correct: No - HL students study three optional themes from the list, while SL students study two.
items.misconception: HL students must cover every optional theme in the syllabus.
items.random_wrong: Optional themes are only studied at Standard Level.
items.why: The HL/SL difference is a common confusion: HL adds one extra option plus the HL core extension, not the whole list.

items.q: Is a hazard the same thing as a disaster?
items.correct: No - a hazard is a potentially damaging event; it becomes a disaster only when it seriously disrupts a vulnerable community that cannot cope using its own resources.
items.misconception: Every earthquake or flood automatically counts as a disaster.
items.random_wrong: A disaster is any event that is reported in the news.
items.why: Risk = hazard x vulnerability: a powerful earthquake in an empty desert is not a disaster, while a moderate one in a vulnerable city can be.

items.q: Does a river's discharge stay the same from source to mouth?
items.correct: No - discharge usually increases downstream as tributaries join and the area being drained grows larger.
items.misconception: Rivers are biggest and most powerful near their mountain source.
items.random_wrong: Discharge is measured in degrees Celsius.
items.why: Students picture dramatic upland torrents and assume more water; in fact velocity and discharge generally rise downstream as channels become more efficient.

items.q: Which of these is one of the IB Geography optional themes?
items.misconception: Global climate - vulnerability and resilience (core unit)
items.why: Correct answer: Geophysical hazards. “Global climate - vulnerability and resilience (core unit)” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In the freshwater theme, what is a drainage basin?
items.correct: The area of land drained by a river and its tributaries
items.random_wrong: The deepest part of the ocean
items.why: It's The area of land drained by a river and its tributaries. “An underground water storage tank” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Hazard risk depends on the hazard event, exposure and what else?
items.why: Correct answer: Vulnerability and capacity to cope. “The Richter scale value alone” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In the tourism theme, carrying capacity refers to what?
items.correct: The maximum visitor numbers a place can support without unacceptable damage
items.misconception: The weight a tour bus can carry
items.random_wrong: The number of hotels in a resort
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The maximum visitor numbers a place can support without unacceptable damage. “The weight a tour bus can carry” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many optional themes does an IB Geography HL student study?
items.why: The real figure is 3 themes. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
